thread.title: Empowering Finnish Women: Skills Training Amid Rising Security Threats from Russia
thread.title_full: Empowering Finnish Women: Skills Training Amid Rising Security Threats from Russia

author: newsdirectory3
title: Empowering Finnish Women: Skills Training Amid Rising Security Threats from Russia
text:
On a military base in western Finland , a group of women in camouflage held a course on surviving without electricity. The course took place after Russia’s invasion of Ukraine raised concerns about potential threats to Finland.
Sari, 42, a participant, felt the need to prepare after witnessing the attack on Ukraine. She emphasizes the importance of having practical skills. About 75 women attended the weekend training, which included lessons on outdoor cooking, first aid, and fire safety. These skills can help in emergencies, whether due to natural disasters or military threats.
Tytti, 36, acknowledged her anxiety about Finland’s relationship with Russia and sees the course as a way to confront her fears. Other participants, ranging in age from 18 to 70, had previously attended shooting courses but preferred to learn unarmed skills.
This training, called Nasta , is organized by the Women’s National Emergency Preparedness Association. After the Ukraine invasion, interest in these courses surged. Finland shares an 830-mile border with Russia, leading to increased vigilance. Courses cover topics such as cybersecurity and wilderness survival.
A Finnish government official criticized suspected sabotage of an underwater cable between Finland and Germany, raising concerns about Russian aggression. Finnish authorities describe Russia as the country’s main security threat, pointing to cyber warfare, disinformation, and possible espionage.
The Nasta program has operated since 1997 and is partially funded by the Finnish Ministry of Defence. Following the invasion of Ukraine in February 2022, demand for these courses rose sharply, as many sought training in self-defense and emergency preparedness.
How can the Women’s National Emergency Preparedness Association ‌help ‌women feel more empowered ​in crisis situations? Interview with Colonel Elisa Lehtonen: Specialist on Civil Preparedness Training⁤ for Women in Finland
NewsDirectory3.com sat down with Colonel Elisa Lehtonen, a leading ⁣specialist on civil emergency preparedness and the head of the⁤ Women’s National Emergency Preparedness Association in Finland. With recent events highlighting the importance of readiness, Colonel Lehtonen shared insights on the recent Nasta training course⁤ held‍ at⁢ a military base in‌ western Finland, aimed at empowering women through practical survival skills.
Q: Colonel Lehtonen, could you explain the motivation ⁢behind the Nasta program and what⁣ it aims to achieve for ​participants?
Colonel Lehtonen: Certainly. The Nasta program was initiated in​ response to increasing concerns about security ‌threats ‌in Europe, particularly following Russia’s invasion of Ukraine. Our⁣ goal is to empower women with essential skills that can be pivotal⁣ during emergencies, whether ‌they arise from‍ natural disasters or geopolitical​ instabilities.⁣ Skills such as outdoor cooking,⁢ first aid, and fire safety⁢ are crucial for fostering​ resilience not just ‌on an individual​ level, but ⁤also in ⁢communities.
Q: What kind of response have you ⁢seen from participants, and what can you‍ tell us about the demographics ⁣of those who joined‌ the⁣ recent course?
Colonel Lehtonen: The response has been overwhelmingly⁢ positive. We had about 75⁣ women, aged ​between 18 and 70,‍ attending the recent⁣ weekend training. It’s fascinating to‌ see such a diverse group⁤ coming together, motivated ⁢by‌ a shared desire⁣ to prepare for unforeseen situations. Many participants, like Sari, expressed that ⁢witnessing current global events spurred‍ them into action to build practical skills. Others, like Tytti, ⁤shared their anxieties regarding regional tensions, ⁤thus viewing this​ training as a method⁤ of confronting and overcoming their fears.
Q: You’ve mentioned⁤ practical skills. How do you convey the importance of these‌ skills ​to someone⁤ who may have previously focused more on armed training?
Colonel Lehtonen: ​ While some women ⁣have indeed ⁤attended shooting courses, the Nasta program emphasizes unarmed survival skills that can be ‌invaluable in various scenarios. We teach that preparedness isn’t solely about physical combat; it’s also about sustaining oneself and ‍others in a crisis—using our knowledge and creativity to navigate challenges without relying on conventional weaponry. Skills like first ‍aid can save lives in emergencies, and outdoor cooking ensures nutritional needs are met when resources are limited.
Q: What have been⁤ some ‍challenges you’ve faced in organizing courses like Nasta, especially considering the sensitive political climate?
Colonel Lehtonen: One of ⁢the main challenges has been addressing the conflicting emotions many women feel about security and preparedness.⁤ While it’s ​important ​to equip them with skills, we also want to ensure ⁤the courses don’t​ instill fear but rather promote empowerment and resilience. ​Engaging participants in​ discussions about⁣ their feelings regarding the current geopolitical climate also fosters a sense of community and support among⁤ attendees.
Q: Looking ahead, what future initiatives can we expect from the Women’s National Emergency ⁣Preparedness Association?
Colonel Lehtonen: We are keen to expand our programs, not only to include more⁣ advanced survival courses but also workshops focusing on mental resilience and community building. Our aim is to create a network of ⁤prepared women⁤ who can support each other during emergencies. We are also looking into collaborations with other organizations to enhance the scope of our training.⁢ The commitment to inclusivity and empowerment is core to our vision moving forward.
Q:​ what⁣ message would you ⁤like to share ‌with the women of Finland regarding preparedness and‍ empowerment?
Colonel Lehtonen: I want to remind all women that preparedness is not just about surviving; it’s about thriving in any circumstance. By gaining ​practical skills and knowledge, you’re not only protecting yourself but also ⁢contributing positively to your ​community. We have the strength to build a safer future together. Don’t ⁢wait for a crisis ⁤to learn—start today!
Colonel Lehtonen’s insights underscore the ​proactive approach taken by women in Finland towards⁣ personal and community resilience ‌as they navigate complex global challenges.
Aksela, a program manager, noted that the surge in course applications resembled a ticket rush for a popular concert. Courses often fill within minutes. Many callers ask about shooting lessons and emergency supplies.
Finnish households are encouraged to have enough supplies for three days in emergencies. Women, particularly those with university backgrounds, comprise a significant portion of the participants. Finland’s history of conflict with Russia informs the seriousness with which citizens view the current situation.
The obligation for military service remains in Finland, contributing to a culture of national preparedness. The Finnish intelligence service warns of Russia’s hybrid warfare tactics, emphasizing the importance of being ready for various threats.
Participants at the Nasta course expressed their motivations for training. Many cited the war in Ukraine as a catalyst for their desire to learn practical skills for crisis situations. The sense of community among the women grew as they camped together and shared their experiences, all while navigating the chill of a Finnish winter night.
